result:
Waiting has a texture, a weight. It’s not simply the absence of action—it’s a substance that fills the room, pressing against your eardrums until you can hear your own pulse. I remember sitting in a train station once, late autumn, the light already leaching out of the sky at four in the afternoon. The bench was cold iron, painted over so many times the layers felt soft, like old skin. A woman near me kept glancing at the arrivals board with that particular tilt of the head that means the minutes have become personal enemies. A child dropped a paper cup and no one picked it up. We were all suspended, our lives paused mid-sentence by a delay announced in a crackling voice that belonged to no one in particular.

In that collective state, something strange happens: you become attentive to everything inconsequential. The way a pigeon’s head jerks, the scuff mark on the tiles shaped like Australia, the exact rhythm of a stranger’s cough. Waiting strips away the pretense of purpose and forces you to inhabit the raw present. It’s uncomfortable precisely because it offers no buffer against yourself. There’s no task to hide behind, no productivity to justify the moment. You’re just there, alive and spectacularly pointless.

And yet, some of my sharpest memories were forged in such gaps. Real living, I think, often seeps in through the cracks we didn’t mean to leave open. We spend so much energy trying to eliminate waiting—faster connections, instant responses, seamless efficiency—but maybe we’re bulldozing the quiet spaces where our minds can finally breathe in their own rhythm. To wait is to admit that we are not the sole authors of our time, and that’s a kind of humility long out of fashion. I wonder what we lose when we cash in every last second of pause for the noise of being busy.

I can choose something interesting, reflective, or creative. I'll aim for exactly around 250 words. That could be poetic and allow for a natural flow of words. I'll count words as I go.
